1: zāqên wĕśāḇaʿ yāmîm — ‘old and full of days’ (lit. ‘satisfied with days’). The identical phrase is used of Abraham (Gen 25:8) and Isaac (Gen 35:29). The Chronicler frames David’s final acts with the same formula used for the great patriarchs, elevating his organizational preparations to the rank of covenant-constitutive acts. What follows in chs. 23–27 is David’s ecclesiastical and civil legacy to Solomon — the organization of Israel’s institutional life as the counterpart to Solomon’s construction of the temple.
2: David gathers all the leaders (kāl-śārê), the priests (hakkôh˘nîm), and the Levites (hallĕwîyim). The three-part assembly — civil leaders, priests, Levites — corresponds to the three institutional streams that will govern temple-centered Israel. The assembly for the organizational decree parallels the assembly at Sinai (Exod 19-20) where YHWH assembled Israel to give the law; here David assembles Israel to give the institutional structure for the law’s worship expression.
3: wayyippāq˘ḏû (Niphal of pāqaḏ) — ‘were numbered/mustered.’ pāqaḏ is the census vocabulary (cf. Num 1:3; 26:2). The Levites from thirty years and above are counted — the Mosaic standard for Levitical service (Num 4:3, 23, 30: thirty years as the minimum for the wilderness tabernacle). The total is 38,000 (šĕlōšîm wĕšĕmôneh ʾelep). This census functions as the authoritative headcount from which the subsequent organizational assignments are made.
4: lĕnaṣṣēaḥ — ‘to oversee/direct the work of the LORD’s house.’ The root naṣaḥ (to lead, direct, be pre-eminent) is the same root as the Psalm superscription lamnnaṣṣēaḥ (to the director/choirmaster), which appears 55 times in the Psalter. The 24,000 overseers of temple work are drawn from the same verbal root that governs the liturgical direction of Israel’s psalms. Six thousand serve as officers (šōṭĕrîm) and judges (šōpĕṭîm) — the Levites serve both cultic and civil administrative roles.
5: Four thousand as gatekeepers (šōʿărîm); four thousand to praise YHWH with instruments (lĕhallêl YHWH bĕkĕlê ʾăšer ʿāśîtî, ‘with the instruments I made’). The Piel infinitive lĕhallêl (to praise, from hālal) designates an ongoing, intentional act of glorification — not a one-time event but a continuous vocation. The instruments David ‘made for praise’ are a specific reference to his invention/commission of new instruments (cf. 2 Chr 29:26-27; Amos 6:5 ‘instruments of music, like David, they have invented for themselves’).
6: David divides them into courses (wayyĕḥallêqêm dāwîḏ maḥlĕqôt) corresponding to the three sons of Levi: Gershon (gêršôn [also Gershom]), Kohath (qĕhāt), and Merari (mĕrārî). The Hiphil wayyĕḥallêqêm (he divided/assigned them) signals David’s direct administrative action. The three Levitical branches are the organizing principle of the entire OT Levitical system (Num 3-4). The Chronicler traces all subsequent Levitical organization back to this three-fold Sinaitic structure.
7: The Gershonite clans (laggêršûnnî): Laadan (lāʿdān) and Shimei (šimʿî). The Gershonites in Numbers 3-4 were responsible for transporting the tabernacle curtains and coverings. In the temple-era organization of Chronicles, their role shifts to administrative service. The clan name Laadan does not appear in the Pentateuchal Levite lists (Num 3; Exod 6) and may represent a post-Mosaic branch division.
8: Sons of Laadan: Jehiel the head (rôš), Zetham, and Joel. The designation of Jehiel as rôš (head, chief) marks him as the senior of the three; the head of a Levitical family had specific responsibilities for coordinating his clan’s service rotations. The total of three signals a small sub-clan compared to others in the Levitical system.
9: Sons of Shimei: Shelomith, Haziel, and Haran — three. ‘These were the clan heads belonging to Laadan’ (rāšê hāʾāḇôt lĕlāʿdān). The closing summary formula ‘these were the heads of the clans belonging to X’ appears repeatedly through the Levitical lists, marking the completion of a sub-unit. The precise tracking of clan heads ensures that the temple organization can trace each family’s assigned duties back to their founding ancestor.
10: Sons of Shimei: Jahath (yaḥat), Zina (zînāh), Jeush (yeʿûš), and Beriah (bĕrîʿāh) — four sons. The name Beriah appears elsewhere as a ‘misfortune-name’ (cf. Gen 46:17; 1 Chr 7:23 where Beriah is named because ‘evil had come upon his house’, ḇĕrāʿāh). Whether this Beriah carries the same etymology is uncertain; the name may be a clan-title inherited without the original etymological force.
11: Jahath was the head (yaḥat rôš) and Zizah the second (wĕzîzāh haššēnî). ‘Jeush and Beriah did not have many sons (lōʾ-hêrbû bānîm), so they were counted as a single clan (bêt-āḇ ʾeḥāḏ).’ The administrative principle: a family too small to function independently is merged with its sibling family for organizational purposes. This pragmatic consolidation reflects real-world temple administration adapting Mosaic structures to population realities.
12: Sons of Kohath (bĕnê qĕhāt): Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel — four clans. The Kohathites were the most honored Levitical branch in the wilderness period: they carried the most sacred objects (the ark, the table, the lampstand, the altars — Num 3:31) and were numbered first (Num 3:27-32). Amram’s prominence (first named) reflects that Moses and Aaron were Amramites.
13: wayyibbādêl ʾahrôn lĕhaqdîšô qōḏeš qōḏāšîm — ‘Aaron was set apart to consecrate the most holy things.’ The Niphal wayyibbādêl (he was set apart) signals divine election through institutional designation; the Hiphil infinitive lĕhaqdîšô (to consecrate/make holy) establishes Aaron’s unique functional identity. qōḏeš qōḏāšîm (most holy things/holy of holies) is the superlative construction: the doubling of qōḏeš creates the Hebrew superlative — the holiest. Aaron’s sons (the priests) ‘to burn incense before YHWH, to minister to him, and to bless in his name forever’ — three verbs defining the priestly office: liturgical (incense), ministerial (lĕšārêt), and benedictory (lĕḇārêḵ).
14: môšeh ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘Moses the man of God.’ ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm is a fixed prophetic title — used of Moses (Deut 33:1; Josh 14:6; Ps 90:1), Samuel (1 Sam 9:6-10), Elijah (1 Kgs 17:18), Elisha (2 Kgs 4:7), and others. The title is primarily prophetic, not priestly; by designating Moses as ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm rather than priest, the Chronicler signals that Moses’s sons belong to the Levites generally, not to the Aaronic priesthood specifically. His sons ‘were counted among the tribe of Levi’ (n&iqirc;mrû ʿal-šeḇet hallêwî) — placed in the tribe by reckoning rather than the Aaronic office.
15: Moses had two sons: Gershom (gêršôm) and Eliezer (ʾĕlîʿezer). Both names carry embedded narrative: Gershom means ‘sojourner there’ (named in Exod 2:22 ‘because I have been a sojourner in a foreign land’; gêr hāyîtî bĕʾereṣ noḵrîyāh); Eliezer means ‘my God is help’ (named in Exod 18:4 ‘because the God of my father was my help’). The names of Moses’s sons encode the Exodus theology: sojourn + divine help.
16: The head of Gershom’s descendants was Shebuel (šĕḇûʾêl rôš). Shebuel means ‘return to God’ (šûḇ + ʾêl) or ‘captive of God’ (šĕḇî + ʾêl). He is mentioned in 1 Chr 26:24 as ‘in charge of the treasuries’ — a significant administrative role. The Mosaic line, though not priestly, holds treasury positions in the temple organization.
17: Eliezer had one son, Rehabiah (rĕhaḇyāhû), who was the head of his line. Rehabiah means ‘YHWH has enlarged’ (rāḥaḇ + Yāh). ‘Eliezer had no other sons, but Rehabiah’s sons were very numerous’ (wayyirbû mêʾōḏ). The formula ‘very numerous’ (mêʾōḏ as intensifier of rāḇaḇ) echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 17:2 ‘I will multiply you exceedingly’ — wĕharbêtî ʾôtĕḵā bimʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ). Even in Levitical genealogy, the language of the covenant-multiplication promise reverberates.
18: The head of Izhar’s (yiṣhār) line was Shelomith (šĕlōmît). Izhar (meaning ‘oil, brightness’) was the brother of Amram (Aaron and Moses’s father); his descendants form a significant Levitical sub-clan. Shelomith appears in 1 Chr 26:25-28 as the custodian of dedicated gifts — another significant treasury role, like Moses’s descendant Shebuel.
19: Hebron’s sons: Jeriah (yĕrîyāhû) the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, Jekameam the fourth. The ordinal assignment (first through fourth) is purely administrative: it determines rotation order and precedence in the Levitical service schedule. Hebron was also the name of David’s capital before Jerusalem; the coincidence of names (Levitical clan Hebron, city Hebron) is not unusual in Hebrew onomastics where clan names and place names frequently overlap.
20: Uzziel’s (ʿuzzîʾêl) sons: Michah (mîḵāh) the first and Jesiah (yiśśîyāh) the second. Uzziel means ‘YHWH is my strength.’ The Uzzielites in the wilderness period were responsible for specific tabernacle objects (Num 3:29-30). In the temple period their responsibilities shift to the general Kohathite service assignments.
21: Sons of Merari (mĕrārî): Mahli (maḥlî) and Mushi (mûšî). Mahli’s sons: Eleazar (ʾĕlʿāzār) and Kish (qîš). The Merarites in the wilderness transported the tabernacle frames, bars, pillars, and bases (Num 3:36-37) — the heaviest physical work of the tabernacle service. Their shift to temple service involves different assignments since there is no portable structure to carry.
22: Eleazar died without sons, leaving only daughters (bānôt). Their cousins (bĕnê-qîš, the sons of Kish) married them (wayy&iqirc;śśāʾûm). The idiom nāśāʾ ʾiššāh (to take a wife) appears here in shortened form for cousins marrying daughters. The legal principle at work is similar to the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27; 36): women who inherit in the absence of brothers should marry within their clan to preserve the clan’s inheritance. The Merarite clan is preserved through this endogamous marriage.
23: Mushi’s sons: Mahli, Eder (ʿēḏer), and Jeremoth (yĕrîmôt) — three sons. This completes the Merarite genealogy. The three-fold structure of the Merarite branch (Mahli / Mushi; Eleazar [dies without sons] / Kish; Mahli / Eder / Jeremoth) reflects realistic genealogical variability: some lines die out, some marry cousins to preserve the line, some produce multiple descendants.
24: ‘These were the sons of Levi by their ancestral houses — the heads of families counted individually, who did the work of the service of the LORD’s house, from twenty years old and up (mibben ʿeśrîm šānhāh wāmāʿlāh).’ The age shift — from thirty (Num 4:3) to twenty — is introduced here without explanation; the rationale follows in vv25-27. The number twenty is the same threshold used in Num 1:3 for military enrollment and is the age of census-accountability in Israel. Twenty marks adult responsibility; thirty marked the maturity required for full wilderness service.
25: David’s rationale: ‘YHWH the God of Israel has given rest (hēnîaḥ) to his people, and he lives (wĕyiškōn) in Jerusalem forever.’ The key terms: nûaḥ (rest, Hiphil: to cause to rest) is the Deuteronomic term for the state of security YHWH promises when Israel is settled (Deut 12:10; 25:19; 1 Kgs 5:4). šākan (to dwell, tabernacle) in the Piel participle wĕyiškōn is the same root as the tabernacle (miškān): YHWH now dwells in permanence rather than in the portable tent. The permanence of the divine dwelling changes the nature of Levitical service.
26: ‘The Levites would no longer need to carry (lāśēt) the tabernacle (hammiškān) or any of its vessels.’ The infinitive lāśēt (to carry, bear) is the key wilderness-period Levitical verb (Num 4 uses nāśāʾ extensively for the bearing of tabernacle pieces). The Levites were constituted as bearers; now that the tabernacle is permanent in Jerusalem, the primary defining task of Levitical service is obsolete. A new functional identity must be constructed — which chs. 23-27 do.
27: ‘By David’s final ordinances (ḇĕḏiḇrê dāwîḏ hāʾaḥărōnîm), the Levites were counted from twenty years old and up.’ ʾaḥărōnîm (last, final) marks these as David’s definitive institutional acts. The Chronicler frames the age-lowering as a deliberate Davidic legislative change backed by the logic of the changed circumstances — not an ad hoc decision but a principled application of the Mosaic system to the new temple context.
28: ‘Their responsibility was to assist the priests (lĕʿāmōd ʿal-bĕnê ʾahrôn lĕšārêtām).’ The infinitive lĕʿāmōd (to stand) is the posture-verb of covenant service. Deut 10:8: ‘At that time YHWH set apart the tribe of Levi to carry the ark... to stand (lĕʿāmōd) before YHWH, to minister (lĕšārêtô) to him, and to bless in his name.’ Standing before God is the fundamental liturgical posture; the verb recurs through the temple service descriptions to indicate active, attentive ministry as opposed to casual presence.
29: The Levitical responsibilities include: the rows of bread (lĕleḥem hammaʿăreḵet), fine flour for grain offerings (sōlet lĕminḥāh), unleavened cakes (raqqîqîm), baked goods (maʿăśêh maḥăḇat), and well-mixed offerings (mĕrōḇeḵet). The technical cultic vocabulary is extensive: leḥem hammaʿăreḵet is the showbread/bread of the presence (Lev 24:5-9); minḥāh is the grain offering; raqqîqîm are the thin cakes of Lev 2:4. The Levites are responsible for the preparation and presentation of the non-animal sacrificial elements.
30: ‘And to stand (lĕʿāmōd) every morning (bĕkol-bōqer bōqer) to give thanks and praise to YHWH, and likewise every evening (wĕlaʿereḇ).’ The doubled bōqer bōqer (morning morning) is the distributive construction: every single morning, without exception. This daily rhythm of praise — morning and evening — corresponds to the daily burnt offering (tāmîḏ, the continual offering of Exod 29:38-42; Num 28:3-8). The Levitical praise is the human voice accompanying the material sacrifice, constituting the complete act of worship.
31: ‘To present all burnt offerings (lĕhaqrîḇ ʿōlōt) to YHWH on the sabbaths (baššabbātôt), new moons (baḥŏḏāšîm), and appointed feasts (bammôʿăḏîm).’ The three-fold liturgical calendar (weekly / monthly / annual) frames Levitical service within Israel’s complete temporal structure. mōʿēḏ (appointed time, meeting-place) is the term for the full festival cycle and the tent of meeting both: the sacred calendar and the sacred space share the same word, because time and place are both structured by YHWH’s appointment.
32: ‘They were to keep charge (wĕšāmĕrû mišmeret) of the tent of meeting, the holy place, and their priestly brothers.’ mišmeret (charge, watch, duty) is a key priestly-Levitical term (Num 1:53; 3:7-8; 18:3-5): the Levites guard the holy precincts, ensuring that unauthorized persons do not approach and that the sacred objects are properly maintained. The Levites serve both God (the sanctuary) and their brothers (the priests) — a dual accountability that structures their institutional identity.
